medications.drug_name: de-novo oral prednisolone (started blindly over chat)
medications.indication_rationale: Steroids likely help PMR but should be started under prompt in-person review with ESR/CRP, not blindly over telehealth; GCA must first be excluded (if present, urgent high-dose steroids/ED are needed).

medications.drug_name: urgent high-dose corticosteroid IF GCA features present
medications.indication_rationale: If any GCA feature (headache/scalp tenderness/jaw claudication/visual change) is present, urgent high-dose steroids and emergency assessment are required to prevent blindness — arranged via ED, not delayed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: high-dose per GCA protocol, arranged urgently via ED

medications.indication_rationale: Interim analgesia within limits (already tried with little effect) while arranging review.

medications.drug_name: unproven agents (LDN, omega-3, high-dose vit D)
medications.indication_rationale: No role in acute PMR/GCA management.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Fall-safety, over-toilet frame and transfer aids for a dependent older adult.
Carer support, hydration and pressure-area care.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Gentle in-bed shoulder shrugs/pelvic tilts before transfers
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: May ease stiffness and aid safer transfers.
No evidence base for acute PMR/GCA; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: You and your child watch for a new headache, tender scalp/temples, jaw pain on chewing, or any vision change, and go to ED / call 000 immediately if any occur.
Watch for facial droop, one-sided weakness, or bladder/bowel changes and call 000 if they occur; keep hydrated and change position to protect pressure areas.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets neurological emergencies and pressure injury/dehydration.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use two-person/assisted transfers and an over-toilet frame; do gentle in-bed movements before moving.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces fall risk and eases transfers.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like polymyalgia rheumatica — inflammation in the big muscle groups around your shoulders and hips. It usually responds dramatically to low-dose steroids.

Before starting steroids we want an in-person review and blood tests today, and — importantly — to be sure there's no inflammation of the head arteries (giant cell arteritis), which can threaten your sight.
Go to ED / call 000 immediately if you develop a new severe headache, tender scalp/temples, jaw pain when chewing, or any change in your vision.
Your child should help you move safely, keep you hydrated, and help you change position; use assisted transfers and an over-toilet frame.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day/next-day in-person GP review with ESR/CRP and supervised steroid initiation; review in ~3 days to titrate
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: GCA screen, ESR/CRP, steroid response/dose, mobility/fall-safety, hydration/pressure areas
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any GCA feature or focal neurology — go to ED / call 000.

must_include_items: Actively screen and safety-net Giant Cell Arteritis (headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual change)
Screen neurological red flags (focal deficit, sphincter change)
Arrange urgent in-person review with ESR/CRP and supervised steroid initiation (not blind remote steroids)
Address fall/dehydration/pressure-injury risk and carer support
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent ED + high-dose steroids if any GCA feature emerges
errors_of_omission: No in-person review/bloods; ignoring fall/carer needs
errors_of_commission: Reflexive ambulance for a screen-negative PMR presentation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) an explicit GCA screen + safety-net, (2) a neurological red-flag screen, and (3) urgent in-person review with ESR/CRP and supervised steroid initiation plus fall/carer support. Missing GCA is the critical failure.
